title: Topical, Transdermal, and Rectal Medication Administration
subtitle: The nurse gives medications by skin and rectal routes safely — wearing gloves for topicals, removing the old patch before a new one and never cutting it, and positioning in Sims' for a retained suppository.

patient.name: Topical / Patch / Rectal Meds
patient.admitDate: The nurse administers topical, transdermal, and rectal medications safely.

nursesNote: The nurse administers medications by the topical, transdermal, and rectal routes after verifying the medication rights. Gloves are worn for topical agents, which are applied in a thin, even layer to clean skin. For a transdermal patch the nurse always removes the previous patch before applying a new one to prevent a double dose, chooses a clean, dry, hairless site, rotates sites to reduce irritation, and never cuts the patch because that alters the dose; the new patch is dated, timed, and initialed, and the old one is folded sticky-sides together and discarded safely. For a rectal suppository the patient is placed in the left lateral (Sims') position; the lubricated suppository is inserted past the internal anal sphincter along the rectal wall and not into a mass of stool, and the patient stays lying for about 15-20 minutes and avoids bearing down so the medication is retained. The nurse documents the removal of the old patch as well as each medication given.
providerOrders: Verify the medication rights before each route
Wear gloves for topical and transdermal medications
Remove the old transdermal patch before applying a new one; never cut a patch
Date, time, and initial the new patch and rotate sites
Give rectal suppositories in Sims' position past the internal sphincter

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about these routes?

opts.text: The medication rights are verified for every route
The old transdermal patch is removed before a new one
Gloves are worn to apply topical and transdermal meds
Transdermal patches may be cut to adjust the dose
A rectal suppository is given in the left lateral position
Topical medication is applied to open skin by default
Leaving the old patch on with a new one can cause an overdose
Rotating patch sites reduces skin irritation
Cutting an extended-release patch can release the drug too fast
A suppository is inserted past the internal sphincter to be retained
Inserting a suppository into stool reduces absorption
Dating and initialing a patch is unnecessary

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order applying a new transdermal patch

opts.text: Verify the rights and don gloves
Remove the old patch and clean the skin
Select a clean, dry, hairless site
Apply the new patch and date/initial it
Discard the old patch safely and document
Remove the previous dose before applying
Position the patient in the left lateral (Sims') position
Never cut the medication to change the dose
Lubricate and insert past the internal sphincter
Rotate sites and choose a hairless area
Have the patient remain lying for 15-20 minutes
Date, time, and initial the medication
Avoid inserting into a mass of stool

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Yesterday's patch is still on as you go to apply a new one
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop and don gloves
Remove the old patch and clean residue from the skin
Assess the patient for signs of overdose
Apply the new patch at a different site and label it
Document removal and report per policy
Removing the old patch before applying a new one
Applying a new patch over the old one
Wearing gloves to apply transdermal medication
Cutting a patch in half to lower the dose
Placing the patient in Sims' position for a suppository
Inserting a suppository into a mass of stool
Dating and initialing the applied patch
Letting the patient stand up right after a suppository
